title: Why we lie to ourselves about how tired we are
summary: Argue that people systematically underestimate or deny their own tiredness, and explain what drives this habit.
topic: Why we lie to ourselves about how tired we are
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) arguing that people regularly deceive themselves about how tired they are. Describe a specific moment when you or someone you know pushed on despite being exhausted. Use present perfect, modal verbs, and at least one concession with 'although' or 'however'.
hintStarters: What puzzles me most about this habit is…
I'd argue that the real problem isn't tiredness itself, but…
If we admitted how tired we were, we might…

hintVocab.meaningVi: cố vượt qua sự kiệt sức
hoạt động khi đã cạn kiệt sức lực
gây tổn hại theo thời gian
nghỉ ngơi mà không cảm thấy có lỗi

sampleText:
A friend of mine once drove four hours from Edinburgh to Manchester after a full day of work, convinced she was fine. She had told herself that the first yawn was just boredom, that the second was the heating in the car, and that stopping was a waste of time. She arrived safely, but she has since admitted that she could not remember the last forty minutes of the drive. Most of us have done something similar — pushed through exhaustion and told ourselves a small, convenient lie.

What puzzles me most about this habit is that tiredness is not subtle. It blurs your vision, slows your reactions, and pulls at the edges of your thinking. Although we know this, we are remarkably good at inventing excuses to keep going. We convince ourselves that we will feel better after coffee, or after the next hour, or after the weekend. The reasons are not mysterious. Many workplaces treat rest as laziness, and a lot of people have grown up hearing that fatigue is something to push through, not something to respect. If we admitted how tired we were, we might look weak, or fall behind, or disappoint someone. So we run on empty and call it dedication.

The problem is that this self-deception takes a toll that compounds over time. A single late night is manageable; six months of chronic tiredness changes how you see everything. I have watched colleagues in offices in Toronto and São Paulo drag themselves through meetings where they could barely form a sentence, not because the work demanded it, but because stopping felt worse than continuing. However, rest is not a reward for finishing — it is part of the process. Until we treat tiredness as a warning sign rather than a character flaw, we will keep lying to ourselves, and the price will be paid quietly, in errors we never notice and conversations we barely remember.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: một lời nói dối nhỏ, tiện lợi
làm mờ tầm nhìn của bạn
làm chậm phản xạ của bạn

sampleAnnotations.phrase: we are remarkably good at inventing excuses
sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: chúng ta rất giỏi bịa ra lý do
hoạt động khi đã cạn kiệt sức lực
gây tổn hại theo thời gian
cố lê bước qua các cuộc họp
kể từ đó đã thừa nhận
coi nghỉ ngơi là lười biếng
được trả giá một cách âm thầm

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect for reflection on past experience
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'She has since admitted that she could not remember…' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành để nhấn mạnh rằng hành động nhìn nhận lại vẫn còn liên quan đến hiện tại, dù sự kiện xảy ra trong quá khứ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'She has since admitted that she could not remember…' uses the present perfect to signal that the act of reflecting is still relevant now, even though the event is in the past.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Concession with 'although'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Although we know this, we are remarkably good at inventing excuses…' dùng 'although' để đưa ra một sự nhượng bộ — thừa nhận điều gì đó đúng trước khi trình bày quan điểm đối lập.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Although we know this, we are remarkably good at inventing excuses…' uses 'although' to make a concession — acknowledging something true before presenting the contrasting point.

sampleTranslationVi:
Một người bạn của tôi đã từng lái xe bốn tiếng từ Edinburgh đến Manchester sau một ngày làm việc đầy đủ, tin rằng mình vẫn ổn. Cô ấy đã tự nhủ rằng tiếng ngáp đầu tiên chỉ là do chán, tiếng ngáp thứ hai là do lò sưởi trên xe, và việc dừng lại là lãng phí thời gian. Cô ấy đến nơi an toàn, nhưng kể từ đó đã thừa nhận rằng cô ấy không nhớ bốn mươi phút cuối của chuyến đi. Hầu hết chúng ta đều đã làm điều gì đó tương tự — cố vượt qua sự kiệt sức và tự nói với mình một lời nói dối nhỏ, tiện lợi.

Điều làm tôi băn khoăn nhất về thói quen này là sự mệt mỏi không hề tinh tế. Nó làm mờ tầm nhìn, làm chậm phản xạ và kéo lê mép tư duy của bạn. Mặc dù chúng ta biết điều này, chúng ta lại rất giỏi bịa ra lý do để tiếp tục. Chúng ta tự thuyết phục bản thân rằng mình sẽ cảm thấy tốt hơn sau cà phê, hoặc sau giờ tiếp theo, hoặc sau cuối tuần. Lý do không khó hiểu. Nhiều nơi làm việc coi nghỉ ngơi là lười biếng, và nhiều người lớn lên khi nghe rằng sự mệt mỏi là thứ phải vượt qua, không phải tôn trọng. Nếu chúng ta thừa nhận mình mệt đến mức nào, chúng ta có thể trông yếu đuối, hoặc tụt lại, hoặc làm ai đó thất vọng. Vì vậy chúng ta hoạt động khi đã cạn kiệt sức lực và gọi đó là sự tận tụy.

Vấn đề là sự tự lừa dối này gây tổn hại tích lũy theo thời gian. Một đêm thức khuya đơn lẻ có thể chịu được; sáu tháng mệt mỏi mãn tính thay đổi cách bạn nhìn mọi thứ. Tôi đã chứng kiến đồng nghiệp ở các văn phòng tại Toronto và São Paulo cố lê bước qua các cuộc họp khi họ hầu như không thể thành câu, không phải vì công việc đòi hỏi, mà vì dừng lại cảm thấy tệ hơn là tiếp tục. Tuy nhiên, nghỉ ngơi không phải là phần thưởng khi hoàn thành — nó là một phần của quá trình. Cho đến khi chúng ta coi sự mệt mỏi là dấu hiệu cảnh báo thay vì khuyết điểm tính cách, chúng ta sẽ tiếp tục tự nói dối, và cái giá sẽ được trả một cách âm thầm, trong những lỗi lầm chúng ta không bao giờ chú ý và những cuộc trò chuyện chúng ta hầu như không nhớ.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly as a concession?
options: Although, we know tiredness is real, so we make excuses.
We know tiredness is real, although because we make excuses.
Although we know tiredness, but we are good at making excuses.
Although we know tiredness is real, we are good at making excuses.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although' + clause, main clause — no extra conjunction needed.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' already signals contrast; don't add 'but' or 'so'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses the present perfect?
options: She has since admitted that she could not remember the last forty minutes.
She have since admitted that she cannot remember the last forty minutes.
She since admitted that she could not remember the last forty minutes.
She has since admit that she could not remember the last forty minutes.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect = has/have + past participle; 'admitted' is the correct past participle of 'admit'.
mcQuestions.hint: Third person singular takes 'has', not 'have', plus past participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the most natural way to express an ongoing toll with a modal.
options: We will keeping lie to ourselves, and the price will paying quietly.
We will keep lying to ourselves, and the price will be paid quietly.
We will kept lie to ourselves, and the price will paid quietly.
We keep lie to ourselves, and the price will be paid quiet.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Will keep + gerund' and 'will be paid' (passive) are both correct structures here.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Keep' is followed by a gerund; passive future is 'will be + past participle'.

options: However rest is not a reward for finishing, it is part of the process.
Rest is not a reward for finishing, however it is part of the process.
However, rest is not a reward for finishing — it is part of the process.
Rest is not a reward however for finishing — it is part of the process.
mcQuestions.explanation: When 'however' starts a sentence, it is followed by a comma.
mcQuestions.hint: 'However' at the start of a sentence needs a comma after it.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses a modal verb to talk about a possible past outcome?
options: She might look weak if she admitted she was tired.
She might looked weak if she admitted tiredness.
She might to look weak if she admitted being tired.
If she admitted she was tired, she might have looked weak.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Might have + past participle' expresses a hypothetical past result; modals take the bare infinitive.
mcQuestions.hint: For a hypothetical past result, use 'might have + past participle'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which relative clause is formed correctly?
options: Errors we never notice and conversations we barely remember.
Errors that we never noticing and conversations we barely remembering.
Errors which we never noticed them and conversations we barely remembered them.
Errors we never to notice and conversations we barely to remember.
mcQuestions.explanation: Relative clauses can omit the pronoun when it is the object: 'errors (that) we never notice'.
mcQuestions.hint: Object relative pronouns can be omitted in informal and semi-formal English.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase best describes a negative effect that builds over time?
options: makes a toll that compounds over time
takes a toll that compounds over time
does a toll that compound over time
takes a toll which compounding over time
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Take a toll' is the correct collocation; 'compounds' agrees with the singular subject 'toll'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Take a toll' is a fixed phrase; check subject–verb agreement on 'compounds'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence is the most appropriate B1 opinion statement?
options: Unless tiredness warning sign, lying will us.
Until tiredness is a warning, we will lied ourselves.
Until we treat tiredness as a warning sign, we will keep lying to ourselves.
We will keep to lie until we treat tiredness like a warning sign.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Until + present simple, future simple' is a standard time clause structure at B1.
mcQuestions.hint: Use present simple in the time clause and future simple in the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses parallel structure correctly?
options: It blurs your vision, slowing your reactions, and pulls at the edges.
It blurs your vision, slows your reactions, and pulling the edges.
It blur your vision, slow your reactions, and pull at the edges.
It blurs your vision, slows your reactions, and pulls at the edges of your thinking.
mcQuestions.explanation: Parallel structure requires the same verb form for each item in a list: 'blurs … slows … pulls'.
mcQuestions.hint: All verbs in a list should share the same form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best expresses a contrast using 'not … but'?
options: Not because the work demanded it, but because stopping felt worse than continuing.
Not because the work demand it, but because stopping felt more worse.
Not because the work demanded it, but stopping felt more bad.
Because not the work demanded it, but stopping felt worse.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Not because … but because …' is a clean parallel contrast at B1.
mcQuestions.hint: Both halves need 'because' and a parallel clause structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which adverb phrase fits naturally in 'The price will be paid ___, in errors we never notice'?
options: quiet
quietly
in quiet
with quiet
mcQuestions.explanation: An adverb ('quietly') modifies the passive verb phrase 'will be paid'.
mcQuestions.hint: You need an adverb (ending in -ly) to modify a verb.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Is there a culture or profession where admitting tiredness is seen as strength rather than weakness? What does that tell us about how tiredness is normally treated?
